title: How to raise chickens and pork appropriately

chunks.title: Challenges of Sourcing Quality Eggs
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses the difficulties in finding eggs from chickens that are raised appropriately, highlighting the importance of foraging in their diets. They share their experience in Costa Rica, where they have found a farmer who raises a better-suited breed of chicken for the environment.
chunks.transcript: that shows the practices of the farmer when they are more species appropriate the animal is healthier and you have a much healthier product in the end this is the reason that I struggle with chickens right and and eggs and I know that on your farm that you had in Texas you were doing it very intentionally but that type of chicken and egg is so hard for me to get even here in Costa Rica people always ask do you eat eggs yes I eat eggs it's so hard for me to get a chicken that's raised the way it's supposed to be raised quote unquote uh a chicken that's actually foraging and you know interestingly I talked to one of the farmers here at the farmers market and one of the things I love about being in Costa Rica is they're they're legit Farmers that are up in the mountains outside of where I live and he has two types of chickens one of them is called caseros and they are a chicken that handles the heat better so they get out of the chicken coup more often and they're basically outside all the time so that egg is going to be much more

chunks.title: Preference for Ruminants
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the benefits of ruminants such as cows, goats, and sheep, which can be raised on grass and have a more suitable diet. The speaker emphasizes the nutritional superiority of grass-fed beef and the challenges associated with raising other animals like pigs and chickens in species-appropriate ways.
chunks.transcript: that just stays in an enclosure even though it's outdoors and it's mostly eating grains I want the chicken to really graze and eat the bugs that it's supposed to eat this is why I really appreciate ruminants and by ruminants I mostly mean cows but sheep lamb uh goats bison these are all ruminants also deer these animals can be you know more scalably produced in a way where they eat grass from start to finish and then they're on rotational grazing that's a that's a very high quality animal and not to be not to be um you know like overly appreciative of cows but I think that if you took a grass-fed cow for me a grass-fed grassfish especially regeneratively raised cow that is a very uniquely nutritious and well- raised animal versus even the best chickens that you're going to get out there right now I just I'm I'm very I'm very cow

chunks.title: Misconceptions About Pasture-Raised Animals
chunks.summary: The speaker critiques the term 'pasture-raised' for chickens and pigs, explaining that their natural behaviors often clash with traditional farming methods. They mention how rooting behaviors in pigs can damage pastures, presenting the complexities of maintaining a pasture while marketing the animals as ethically raised.

chunks.title: Feeding Challenges for Farm Animals
chunks.summary: The speaker recounts their experience of raising pigs, emphasizing the significant amount of feed required and the difficulties in replicating a natural diet on farms. They discuss how domesticated animals often cannot exhibit their natural behaviors due to space and feed limitations.
chunks.transcript: in commercial pork that's on a concrete slab that has nothing to root but at the same time we we have this class of animals that were actually eating more of as breed Meats being demonized saturated fats being demonized I got I had eight pigs it was eight tons of feed to raise out eight pigs I got the truckload of the feed to raise these eight eight pigs my [ __ ] mind was blown mad I was like it was on a semi truck like bed bed truck I had it like took me about three hours to get my tractor for each pallet eight of them I put the forklift in I went back and my tractor went like this and slammed down because of how heavy they were and that was just to raise eight pigs that's it that's crazy and and these were like the the best I sprouted the grain I had two separate groups we can talk about some of the finds if you're interested in that but they were in like we had just cleared out some underbrush of yopon which grows a lot in the forest in Texas and put them in there cleared up dig up the roots so they had access to root like they should but seeing the the amount of food I had to give them daily that was not inherent in the environment mind-blowing and if I would have let them go in the same amount of space they would have died they would have been enough food because like a wild hog has hundreds of Acres of access you can't put a pig in in somewhere and have it exhibit a species appropriate behavior on an actual Farm as far as I'm concerned it's very hard and so people

chunks.title: Importance of Grasslands for Cattle
chunks.summary: The benefits of raising cattle on natural grasslands are highlighted, as the speaker discusses their visit to some exemplary farms in Australia. They reflect on the superior farming methods that support the health of grass-fed cattle and the potential nutritional benefits for those who consume such products.
chunks.transcript: and this is a more eloquent answer that you've given than I've typically responded with but the idea is yeah it's it's hard it's just harder to get these these Foods in a species appropriate diet in a species appropriate way and this is one of the things that I think is so great about cattle and ruminants is that they're meant to be on grasslands and that's why I like eating them because there are scalable operations in place I mean we can talk about it when we talk about lineage I went to Australia and saw some of the farming by Melbourne in Victoria and it was the best farming I've ever seen I I I literally went to Farms that were on the ocean I mean it's like in the United States this or anywhere else in the world this uh this type of land would be condos and high-rise hotels but for some reason in the south of Victoria in Australia it's a freaking farm and the farm like backs up to the ocean and so you're you're you're feeling the ocean breeze the grass is super green uh and the cows are just there with the cows have like the this million dollar view to the ocean South to Tasmania in Australia and there's so much amazing farming there that can happen for these ruminants because grasslands and cattle

chunks.title: Mineral Deficiency in Farmed Animals
chunks.summary: The speaker raises concerns about the mineral deficiencies in cattle raised on previously farmed monocrops, pointing out the need for additional supplements in their diets. They express interest in how oceanic minerals might play a role in improving nutrient availability for grazing cattle.
chunks.transcript: really interesting that I would like to see some research on is how mineral deficient even cattle that are raised on continuously grazed pastures the if the fields have ever been farmed before monocrops have effectively pulled all the minerals out of the soil so even if you restore the pasture a lot of times you don't get mineral release from the rocks in the soil and so add things like red Min sea salt and get a lot of that's why cows have salt licks things like that but I'm wondering how much comes off of the ocean minerals oh yeah that just immediately plants straight into the grass the cattle eat it because I

chunks.title: Desire for Thoughtful Food Alternatives
chunks.summary: In the concluding remarks, the speaker discusses their motivation to create better food options that prioritize nutrient quality and selective ingredient sourcing. They express frustration over the lack of awareness regarding food contents and the importance of thoughtfulness in the production of animal-based diets.
chunks.transcript: you sent of the lushness look like basically Hawaii yeah and these animals are just chowing down on super green grass and we've I mean we can get into sourcing stuff in a little bit about us versus Australia and supply chain and all type of stuff but I think that there's a negative stigma on New Zealand and Australia from a grazing thing because I think people just want everything American made but I think that's far more complex than that uh but to get back on track there's a lot of things that should be in food a lot of things that shouldn't be in food no one talks about it no one's thinking about this when they're making a product and this is the I think the the core thing that has been driving me forward over this like two plus year journey to get this out is how can we start making alternatives for what people are reaching for for convenience with a thoughtful manner of what is in here what is not in here and
